title: Extracting extrapolative beliefs from market prices: An augmented present-value approach
abstract: We propose a latent-variables approach to recover extrapolative beliefs from asset prices. We estimate a present-value model of the price–dividend ratio of the market that embeds both return extrapolation and cash-flow extrapolation, alongside discount rates and rational expectations of dividend growth. This approach allows us to measure extrapolation bias without having to rely on survey data, and it inherently guarantees that the researcher focuses on a set of beliefs that matter for price formation. We show that extrapolative beliefs extracted from prices are highly correlated with surveys and that survey-based and price-based extrapolative beliefs share similar predictive properties for future returns, with the former improving upon the latter.

author.Stefano Cassella: Tilburg University, Warandelaan 2, Tilburg, 5037 AB, The Netherlands
author.Te-Feng Chen: Hong Kong Polytechnic University, 11 Yuk Choi Rd, Hong Kong, China

article.1. Introduction: Using survey expectations to elicit investor beliefs, recent work shows widespread evidence that investors extrapolate past investment performance. In particular, Greenwood and Shleifer (2014) document that survey expectations of future returns rise on the back of good stock market returns (i.e., return extrapolation), while Bordalo et al. (2019), De la O and Myers (2021), and Nagel and Xu (2022) show evidence consistent with investors’ expectations of future fundamentals rising on the back of strong recent fundamentals (i.e., cash-flow extrapolation).1Extrapolation of stock-market performance is likely to result in biases in expectations since, in the data, there is only a weak relation between the past and future performance of the market (e.g., Barberis 2018). Following this insight, recent theory work has demonstrated that extrapolation bias can have a sizable impact on stock prices and can help reconcile a number of stylized facts about the stock market (Barberis et al., 2015, Cassella and Gulen, 2018, Choi and Mertens, 2019, Jin and Sui, 2022, Atmaz et al., 2024).While the availability of survey data on investor beliefs has shed light on the impact of extrapolation on investor beliefs, the use of survey data to test theories of biased beliefs has its limitations. First, researchers need comprehensive and preferably long-running surveys, which are lacking at present. Second, surveys only rarely allow to link respondents’ expectations to their holdings or trading behavior, and survey coverage is limited to a selected subpopulation of the market. This makes it difficult to draw strong inferences on the extent to whichextrapolation bias affects investor behavior.2 Third, several researchers (e.g., Prescott, 1977, Lamont, 2003, Cochrane, 2011) have criticized the use of surveys in testing expectations formation on the basis of surveys being noisy and survey responses being sensitive to how survey questions are framed.3 Fourth, concerns regarding measurement aside, if arbitrageurs act swiftly to profit from investors’ biases, extrapolation may be widespread and yet have little impact on asset prices. Fifth, as of yet, return extrapolation and cash-flow extrapolation have largely been studied separately, possibly owing to the fact that surveys often only elicit either return or cash-flow expectations. As a result, it is unclear what the relative contribution of cash-flow extrapolation and return extrapolation is to equilibrium asset prices. Overall, the extent to which extrapolation bias truly matters for asset prices is still an open empirical question.In this paper, we overcome the challenges faced in prior work and assess the joint importance of cash-flow extrapolation and return extrapolation for asset prices by structurally estimating an augmented version of the present-value identity. In our present-value model, we allow the price–dividend ratio of the market to respond to variation in risk preferences as well as variation in rational expectations of dividend growth, as is standard in the literature (Campbell and Shiller, 1988, Van Binsbergen and Koijen, 2010). The novelty of our approach is that we also allow for fluctuations in asset valuation to stem from return extrapolation and cash-flow extrapolation.Our empirical analysis builds on earlier work by Van Binsbergen and Koijen (2010) (henceforth VBK). They use standard Kalman filtering techniques to structurally estimate a model of stock market valuation where variation in the market price–dividend ratio is due to both the time variation in the discount rate and dividend growth expectations. We conjecture that the valuation of the stock market can also emanate from extrapolative beliefs held by some investors in the market. We thus augment the original VBK framework by modeling the variation in the price–dividend ratio as dependent on not only rational investors’ discount rates and cash-flow growth expectations but also on extrapolators’ sentiment about stocks.Like prior research (Greenwood and Shleifer, 2014, Barberis et al., 2015), we model extrapolative beliefs of future stock returns as a weighted sum of past stock returns with decaying weights. Similarly, we model cash-flow extrapolative expectations following the literature as a weighted sum of past dividend-growth realizations (e.g., Nagel and Xu 2022).The intuition for why our structural model is able to simultaneously identify cash-flow extrapolation (henceforth W) and return extrapolation (henceforth S) in the data is simple: while both forms of expectations are weighted sums of past market performance, they are ultimately weighted sums of different quantities. Similarly, the time series of rational expectations of growth (henceforth g) can be separated from extrapolative beliefs since g captures investors’ forward-looking expectations of future dividend growth. Finally, the discount rate (henceforth μ) takes the role of a residual in our decomposition since it is used to match the variation in the price–dividend ratio of the market that cannot be accounted for by either extrapolative beliefs or rational expectations of dividend growth.4In our decomposition, the inclusion of other traditional drivers of stock valuations, such as discount rates and rational expectations of dividend growth alongside extrapolative beliefs, helps to further strengthen the identification of extrapolative expectations in the data. It is important to explicitly model rational expectations of dividend growth alongside cash-flow extrapolation because a rise in dividends that causes cash-flow extrapolative expectation to rise is also likely to coincide with a positive shock to future dividend-growth expectations. Thus, explicitly controlling for rational cash-flow expectations in the model frees the estimate of cash-flow extrapolation from the confounding role of rational expectations of growth in fundamentals. Similarly, a stock price rise that leads to an increase in extrapolative expectations can coincide with a decline in discount rates, so that without modeling discount rates explicitly, one has to worry about over-stating the role of extrapolative expectations for asset prices. The joint modeling of S and μ helps to deal with this concern and thus helps improve the identification of the different forces at play in shaping asset prices.Given the novelty of our approach, we perform an extensive analysis to validate our model. Multiple independent pieces of evidence indicate that our model is indeed able to estimate at once the aforementioned drivers of asset prices. First, we conduct an extensive simulation analysis aimed at investigating how well the model can identify the four latent variables of interest for different values of the model parameters. We find strong evidence that the model is very successful in recovering the latent variables for a large set of parameters. Second, while the extrapolative beliefs in our model are consistent with theoretical models of extrapolation, we acknowledge that our model estimates these time series with the aim of matching asset prices rather than beliefs. This raises the question of whether our price-based extrapolation metrics indeed match the dynamics of extrapolative beliefs from survey data. To answer this question, we first compare raw survey expectations of returns (cash-flows) with our structural estimates and find an encouraging positive correlation of 25% (50%). We then note that survey expectations need not capture just extrapolative beliefs.5 Therefore, we perform a more informative comparison between the extrapolative component of surveys and our estimates. Our return-extrapolation measure has a 70% correlation with return-extrapolative beliefs extracted from surveys, whereas it has a correlation that is close to zero with surveys of cash-flow growth expectations. Similarly, our cash-flow extrapolation time series shares a correlation of 50% with cash-flow extrapolation from surveys and only a small correlation of 10% or less with return-extrapolation data from surveys.6 The fact that the model can recover beliefs in a way that is consistent with survey data is important in that it lends credence to our approach. Moreover, since our measures are extracted from asset prices, this result also provides support for behavioral theories looking to connect fluctuations in asset prices to extrapolative beliefs.In the last part of the paper, we use our estimated time series of investor beliefs about returns and cash flows to conduct aggregate return predictability tests. Since our econometric approach does not aim, like recent work involving machine learning techniques, to build new and robust predictors of stock returns, the goal of this part of the analysis is not to break new ground in the aggregate time-series predictability literature. Rather, the primary purpose of the predictability tests we conduct is to compare the predictive performance of surveys-based extrapolation metrics with our structurally estimated beliefs time series. If our structural approach is successful at measuring extrapolative beliefs rather than other drivers of asset prices, we expect surveys and structurally estimated beliefs metrics to exhibit similar predictive abilities. Indeed, we find similar predictive properties for survey-based extrapolation and our extrapolation metrics, both in-sample and out-of-sample. As a matter of fact, in the in-sample tests, price-based extrapolative beliefs have a stronger predictive power than survey-based metrics based on the size of the predictive coefficient, the statistical significance of the predictability coefficients, and the R2 of the predictive regressions. The improved predictability that is observed using price-based extrapolative beliefs is particularly evident at short and intermediate horizons, while survey-based metrics exhibit somewhat better predictive power at a longer horizon of three years, making the overall predictive ability of two sets of metrics for extrapolation comparable. Out-of-sample tests, either based on Campbell and Thompson (2008) out-of-sample R2 or Clark and West (2007) statistic, deliver similar insights.Our predictive tests also serve a secondary purpose: both theories of cash-flow extrapolation and return extrapolation predict that when extrapolative expectations rise, future returns will be lower. One way of assessing whether return or cash-flow extrapolation matters for asset prices is to compare the ability of these two belief systems to predict future stock returns. In principle, this type of comparison could be done using surveys.7 However, different surveys collect return expectations and cash-flow expectations, often using different types of survey participants, different sample sizes, and different sample periods. All these differences lead to the possibility that differences in the predictive ability of survey-based return- and cash-flow extrapolation are due to differences in the quality of the data and could not appropriately portray the role of the two forms of extrapolation for asset prices. Our empirical approach allows us to circumvent these issues in that we estimate both return extrapolation and cash-flow extrapolation jointly as part of a unified econometric approach. When comparing the predictive ability of cash-flow extrapolation and return extrapolation using our metrics, we find that both share a similar ability to predict future returns, and each has a marginal predictive power for future returns vis-a-vis the other.All in all, our empirical evidence points to a great degree of similarity between existing survey-based extrapolation metrics and our new price-based extrapolation time series. As such, this evidence lends credibility to our new approach, and it reinforces the notion that surveys elicit beliefs that matter for asset prices. While our results indicate the survey-based approach and our structural approach provide answers that are in line with each other, we argue that a cautious interpretation of our results is in order. Simply put, survey data offers direct insights into belief formation, whereas recovering beliefs from market prices as we do constitutes an indirect approach. Our approach to recovering extrapolation asks what is the combination of past return and cash-flow realizations that best matches variation in asset prices rather than what is the combination of such realizations that drives beliefs. Therefore, we argue that both surveys and asset prices should be used jointly in future work looking to extend the framework presented in this paper.Our paper contributes to two strands of literature. Our first contribution is methodological, as we show how to extend a standard present-value model of asset prices to incorporate behavioral factors. In this regard, we contribute to a growing literature that uses structural estimation in asset pricing to extract latent quantities of interest (e.g., Van Binsbergen and Koijen, 2010, Koijen and Yogo, 2019, Gabaix and Koijen, 2020). Two features mark our new present-value framework. First, we propose a new guess-and-verify approach to solving for market equilibrium that complements the VBK solution and makes the inclusion of additional state dynamics straightforward in a present-value relation. Second, our econometric specification allows us to assess the differential impacts of extrapolators versus rational traders, capturing the main insight in theoretical behavioral models. We believe our framework can be extended to test other theories of belief formation and can potentially spur a new stream of academic research.Our second contribution is to a longstanding debate on the role of irrational beliefs for asset prices. In traditional models of financial markets, asset prices reflect only rational expectations and irrational investors are irrelevant for prices due to the presence of deep-pocketed arbitrageurs.8 In contrast to this view, behavioral finance posits that investors’ biased beliefs do impact asset prices (e.g., Shleifer, 2000, Barberis and Thaler, 2005, Barberis, 2018, Bordalo et al., 2024). The recent steps taken by empirical research in the field to directly investigate the role of biases for price formation involve survey data, but researchers worry about the quality of the data and are limited by the sparse nature of surveys. We avoid these concerns and offer a new structural approach to extracting information on biases in beliefs directly from asset prices. Our approach inherently captures biases to the extent that they matter in equilibrium.The rest of the paper is structured as follows. Section 2 presents our structural framework and estimates the model using US data. In Section 3, we provide evidence to help validate our approach. Section 4 presents possible model extensions. Section 5 concludes.
article.2. A model of market valuation with extr: 2.1. The rational benchmark: Van Binsbergen and Koijen (2010)We first describe the model in Van Binsbergen and Koijen (2010) (henceforth VBK), which we treat as the rational benchmark that motivates our framework. Let rt+1=log(Pt+1+Dt+1Pt) denote the log return of the market, pdt+1=log(Pt+1Dt+1) denote the log price–dividend ratio (hereafter pd ratio), and Δdt+1=log(Dt+1Dt) denote the log dividend growth rate. VBK model the expected return (μt≡Et(rt+1)) and the expected dividend growth rate (gt≡Et(Δdt+1)) as latent variables that follow separate AR(1) processes: (1)μt+1=μ0+ρμ(μt−μ0)+ɛμ,t+1,(2)gt+1=g0+ρg(gt−g0)+ɛg,t+1, where μ0 (g0), ρμ (ρg), and ɛμ,t+1 (ɛg,t+1) are the unconditional mean, the persistence parameter, and the innovation to the expected return (the expected dividend growth) process, respectively.Using the Campbell and Shiller (1988) approximation, the log return can be linearized as: (3)rt+1=κ+ρpdt+1−pdt+Δdt+1,where ρ=exp(pd¯)1+exp(pd¯), κ=log(1+exp(pd¯))−ρpd¯, and pd¯ denotes the unconditional mean of the log price–dividend ratio. Iterating the Campbell–Shiller approximation over the log price–dividend ratio and taking conditional expectation at time t, it follows that: (4)pdt=Et[∑j=0∞ρj(κ+Δdt+1+j−rt+1+j)],(5)=A+Bg(gt−g0)−Bμ(μt−μ0), where A=κ+g0−μ01−ρ, Bμ=11−ρρμ, and Bg=11−ρρg. As such, the log price–dividend ratio is linear in the two state variables (i.e., μt and gt), and loads positively on gt and negatively on μt.Eq. (5) is the fundamental equation in VBK that links the observable price–dividend ratio to the two latent state variables. It states that the variation in the price–dividend ratio is completely driven by the variation in the discount rate and the variation in the expected dividend growth rate (and possibly their covariance). Note that there is a one-to-one mapping between (B1,B2) and (ρμ,ρg). As a result, an alternative approach to derive the VBK representation is to first specify the log price–dividend ratio as a linear function of the two latent state variables and then derive the dynamics of expected returns through the Campbell–Shiller decomposition. In the next section, we take this alternative approach to derive the market equilibrium in the presence of extrapolators.2.2. A present-value model with extrapolative beliefsIn this section, we extend the present-value model described in the previous section by incorporating extrapolative beliefs, which are studied in Greenwood and Shleifer (2014), Barberis et al. (2015), Cassella and Gulen (2018), and Nagel and Xu (2022). Our primary focus on return extrapolation as an irrational belief formation process is warranted by the evidence of its pervasiveness in the laboratory and in the field (e.g., De Bondt and Thaler, 1985, De Bondt, 1993, Vissing-Jorgensen, 2003, Nagel, 2012, Dominitz and Manski, 2011, Amromin and Sharpe, 2014, Greenwood and Shleifer, 2014, Cassella and Gulen, 2018, Malmendier et al., 2020). More recent literature also points to the extrapolation of past cash flows as a source of incorrect expectations (e.g., Bordalo et al., 2019, De la O and Myers, 2021, Nagel and Xu, 2022). Following this literature, we present a model that allows for both cash flow extrapolation and return extrapolation, as well as rational dividend growth expectations and time-varying risk preferences, i.e., a time-varying discount rate.2.2.1. Model assumptionsTo simplify our presentation, we deamean all variables and present our model economy in terms of demeaned quantities (denoted with a hat). There are some “rational traders” in the economy who trade a risky asset whose supply is fixed as in the rational benchmark of Van Binsbergen and Koijen (2010). Following Barberis et al. (2015), we assume that there are “return extrapolators” in the economy who form beliefs of future market returns based on past stock market returns. Further, following Nagel and Xu (2022), we assume that there are also “cash flow extrapolators” in the economy who form beliefs of future fundamentals based on past fundamentals.9We first specify the dynamics of the beliefs of return extrapolators. Following prior work, we model the (demeaned) time-series of extrapolators’ return expectation (Sˆt) as follows (e.g., Greenwood and Shleifer, 2014, Barberis et al., 2015): (6)Sˆt+1=(1−βS)Sˆt+βSrˆt+1,where βS is the weighting parameter. We can rewriteSˆt=βS∑j=0∞(1−βS)jr̂t−j, where Sˆt is a weighted average of all past returns. A higher βS indicates more weight is placed on more recent returns.We then specify the dynamics of the beliefs of cash-flow extrapolators, and we model the (demeaned) time-series of extrapolators’ cash-flow expectation (wˆt) as follows (e.g., Nagel and Xu 2022): (7)wˆt+1=(1−βw)wˆt+βwΔd̂t+1where βw is the weighting parameter. Similarly to what we point out for return extrapolation, one can write wˆt=βw∑j=0∞(1−βw)jΔd̂t−j, where wˆt is a weighted average of all past dividend growth rates. A lower βw implies more weights on the distant realizations of past dividend growth.10Rational traders hold time-varying preferences for risk. Similar to the specification in (1) of VBK, we model the (demeaned) variation in rational investors’ required compensation for risk via an autoregressive process that tracks time-varying discount rates: (8)μˆt+1=ρμμˆt+ɛμ,t+1,where ρμ is the persistence parameter. μˆt can be interpreted as the expected market return in the absence of extrapolators. However, this quantity will differ from actual expected returns (i.e., Et(rˆt+1)) when extrapolators are present in the market. Hereafter, to better distinguish μˆt from Et(rˆt+1), we refer to μˆt as the discount rate and Et(rˆt+1) as the (overall) expected return for the market.Another way to distinguish μˆt from the overall expected return of the market is through a frequency decomposition. Because μˆt is highly persistent and can be thought of as the required rate of return for long-term buy-and-hold investors, it is the primary driver of returns in the long run (e.g., over five years). In contrast, the one-year-ahead overall expected market return Et(rˆt+1) is relatively short-run and, therefore, will be affected (or “contaminated”) by extrapolators’ sentiment. Our formulation succinctly captures this distinction in the investment horizon by modeling the discount rate as a self-contained process. While alternative formulations are possible (e.g., directly modeling Et(rˆt+1) as the “discount rate”), they unnecessarily complicate the analysis as well as the intuition because, due to the dependence of extrapolation on returns, alternative formulations imply the reliance of discount rates on extrapolators’ sentiment. To maintain model parsimony and ease model interpretation, we focus on our current formulation.Next, the (demeaned) expected dividend growth rate (gˆt) is assumed to follow an AR(1) process: (9)gˆt+1=ρggˆt+ɛg,t+1,where the model parameter ρg and the shock ɛg,t+1 carry the same interpretations as those in (2) of VBK. The (demeaned) realized dividend growth rate is assumed to follow: (10)Δd̂t+1=gˆt+ɛd,t+1.Note that the four state variables Sˆt+1, wˆt+1, gˆt+1, and μˆt+1 are assumed to only depend on their lagged values. One can easily extend our model by assuming a general vector-autoregression (i.e., VAR), where state variables may load on other lagged state variables. We focus on our specification to maintain model parsimony (given we have limited time series data to estimate our model) and to achieve model identification. Also note that we model the three shocks in the model (i.e., ɛμ,t+1, ɛg,t+1, and ɛd,t+1) as homoscedastic normal shocks that are cross-sectionally independent. We do not allow shock correlations, again to maintain model parsimony. The standard deviations for these shocks are given by σμ, σg, and σd, respectively.Both types of extrapolators trade in the direction of their beliefs so that when past aggregate returns (cash-flow growth) are higher, return-extrapolators (cash-flow extrapolators) become optimistic and increase their demand for stocks. Faced with extrapolators’ irrational demand for stocks and with prices that are higher than justified by fundamentals, rational traders could, in principle, counter the mispricing generated by extrapolators by shorting the overpriced asset, thus leading prices to immediately revert to the value that is warranted by fundamentals. However, rational traders who understand the law of motion of extrapolative beliefs anticipate that bullish extrapolative expectations are likely to persist in the near future. As a result, rational market participants only partially counter the overpricing caused by either type of extrapolators. In equilibrium, the presence of over-optimistic extrapolators generates over-pricing.11In Barberis et al. (2015), an increase in return extrapolators’ sentiment S generates variation in the price–dividend ratio of the market. Similarly, Nagel and Xu (2022) propose that cash-flow extrapolators’ optimism about stocks can lead to fluctuations in the valuation of the market. We combine this behavioral view about the price–dividend ratio of the market with the standard present-value decomposition and postulate the following additive linear structure of the (demeaned) pd ratio as: (11)pd̂t=−Bμμˆt+Bggˆt+BSSˆt+Bwwˆt,where the loading coefficients are either constrained or to be estimated, as we will specify later. All coefficients Bμ, Bg, BS, and Bw will be shown to be positive. As such, −Bμ captures the negative contemporaneous correlation between the discount rate μˆt and the pd ratio pd̂t.12Eq. (11) is our main econometric specification for the impact of extrapolators’ sentiment on asset prices. The sign and the magnitude of such impact are captured by the coefficients BS and Bw, which will be estimated empirically. These coefficients determine the direction and the extent to which extrapolative beliefs affect the valuation of the stock market. When both BS and Bw are zero, our decomposition of the pd ratio coincides with the one in VBK, where asset prices are purely driven by discount rates and expectations of dividend growth, and extrapolation has no impact on equilibrium prices. Conversely, the finding that BS and Bw are different from zero and positive, as the estimation results presented later indicate, makes (11) consistent with theories of asset prices with return and cashflow extrapolation (e.g., Barberis et al., 2015, Nagel and Xu, 2022). In these theories, extrapolators’ sentiment leads to a deviation of stock market valuation from the level that is warranted by fundamentals. As the earlier Eq. (6) shows, this deviation is fueled by past stock returns or past dividend-growth realizations. High recent stock market performance causes extrapolators’ sentiment to become excessively optimistic and stock prices to become too high relative to a rational benchmark. Similarly, a streak of negative news about the market leads to pessimism among extrapolators, which in turn pushes prices too low relative to what would be observed in the absence of extrapolators. Importantly, our formulation of extrapolative sentiment in (6) indicates that sentiment displays an autoregressive behavior, such that optimistic (pessimistic) extrapolative expectations today can be predicted to correct in the future, leading to implications for time-series predictability that are the subject of empirical tests later in the paper.Our formulation for the pd ratio in (11) posits a linear relation between the price–dividend ratio and extrapolators’ sentiment. This relation suggests that one could study the impact of extrapolators’ sentiment on prices by simply regressing the price–dividend ratio on a weighted sum of past returns. However, we argue that this procedure is not appropriate and can generate biased estimates of the impact of extrapolation on asset prices. The reason is that such a simple procedure would suffer from an omitted variable problem because the other latent variables, such as the discount rate and expected dividend growth, would be absent from the regression. For this reason, we focus on the structural approach in our paper.2.2.2. Solving the model economyWe take a different approach from VBK to derive the market equilibrium. First, given the postulated solution for the pd ratio, we derive the implied return dynamics (as perceived by rational traders) through the Campbell–Shiller approximation. Next, we use the implied return dynamics to succinctly represent our model economy within a state-space framework. Lastly, we use the state-space representation to confirm our solution for the pd ratio.Using Campbell–Shiller decomposition (3), demeaned realized return equals rˆt+1=ρpd̂t+1−pd̂t+Δd̂t+1. By substituting in the postulated solution for the pd ratio and the expression for Δd̂t+1 (i.e., (10)), we have: rˆt+1=ρ[−Bμμˆt+1+Bggˆt+1+BSSˆt+1+Bwwˆt+1]−[−Bμμˆt+Bggˆt+BSSˆt+Bwwˆt]+Δd̂t+1.Note that the left-hand side return at t+1 is not exogenously determined by the right-hand side variables. The reason is that Sˆt+1 itself, being extrapolators’ sentiment at the end of the time period, depends on market return over this period through Eq. (6). In other words, return extrapolators observe the most recent market return and update their extrapolative beliefs accordingly. Solving rˆt+1 from the above equation, we can express rˆt+1 in terms of time t state variables and innovations as: (12)rˆt+1=(1−ρBSβS)−1[Bμ(1−ρρμ)]︸λμμˆt+(1−ρBSβS)−1[1+ρBwβw−Bg(1−ρρg)]︸λggˆt+(1−ρBSβS)−1[−BS(1−ρ+ρβS)]︸λsSˆt+(1−ρBSβS)−1[−Bw(1−ρ+ρβw)]︸λwwˆt+(1−ρBSβS)−1[(1+ρBwβw)ɛd,t+1+ρBgɛg,t+1−ρBμɛμ,t+1]︸ɛr,t+1, which implies that rˆt+1=Et(rˆt+1)+ɛr,t+1, where (13)Et(rˆt+1)=λμμˆt+λggˆt+λSSˆt+λwwˆt.We interpret (13) by comparing it to VBK. In VBK, the (demeaned) expected market return is simply μˆt. In our model, expected market returns load on μˆt with a coefficient of (1−ρBSβS)−1, in addition to Bμ(1−ρρμ), which is later set to 1 to achieve model identification. This coefficient is larger than one so as to reflect the amplification of discount rate shocks caused by the presence of extrapolators in the market.The intuition is as follows. In a market with no extrapolators, BS=0 and Bw=0, so that a one-unit increase in discount rates μˆt raises the expected market return by the same unit. This implies, like in VBK, Bμ=11−ρρμ, and Bg=11−ρρg. So, in our estimation, we fix Bμ and Bg accordingly. The presence of return extrapolators in the market exacerbates market movements in response to changes in discount rates. This is because an increase (decline) in discount rates today lowers (increases) contemporaneous prices and returns, leading to pessimistic (optimistic) extrapolative expectations and, hence, even lower (higher) prices and returns today. Therefore, the equilibrium outcome is a (1−ρBSβS)−1-unit (which is larger than 1) change in expected market return following a unit change in discount rates. Note the same amplification mechanism applies to the other state variables (i.e., gˆt, Sˆt, and wˆt).The coefficient λS in Eq. (12) captures the link between return extrapolators’ sentiment and future returns. Besides the amplification effect (i.e., (1−ρBSβS)−1) discussed above, two other offsetting effects contribute to λS. The first effect is quantified by −BS, later estimated to be negative, and captures the fact that when current extrapolators’ beliefs are high, they push up current prices, implying a lower return in the future. The second effect is quantified by BSρ(1−βS), later estimated to be positive, and captures the fact that due to extrapolators’ persistent beliefs, a higher current bias raises the next period bias by a factor of (1−βS), thereby increasing the next period pd ratio by a factor of BS(1−βS). Through the Campbell–Shiller decomposition (3), the corresponding increase in expected return is BSρ(1−βS). The negative current effect and the positive future effect negate each other, leading to a negative effect overall.The coefficient λw in Eq. (12) captures the link between cash-flow extrapolators’ sentiment and future returns. On the one hand, high cash-flow extrapolation raises prices today, leading to lower returns in the future as cash-flow extrapolation reverts to the mean. On the other hand, high cash flow extrapolation today leads prices to stay high in the future through the persistence of this latent process. The overall relationship between w and future returns is negative. The coefficient λg is positive in our model due to the presence of cash-flow extrapolators: as expectations of growth in fundamentals rise, we expect cash-flow extrapolation to rise in the future, leading to a positive relation between g and future returns.Since dividend growth in our model is exogenous, the dynamics of cash-flow extrapolation are already fully described by Eq. (7). Instead, return extrapolation and asset prices are contemporaneously determined. Thus, to obtain the dynamics of return extrapolation given (12), we substitute out market returns in (6) and write down the dynamics of return extrapolators’ sentiment in terms of lagged state variables and innovations: (14)Sˆt+1=(1−βS)Sˆt+βS[λμμˆt+λggˆt+λSSˆt+λwwˆt]+βSɛr,t+1=(1−βS(1−λS))Sˆt+βS[λμμˆt+λggˆt+λwwˆt]+βS(1−ρBSβS)−1[(1+ρBwβw)ɛd,t+1+ρBgɛg,t+1−ρBμɛμ,t+1].Eq. (14) makes two things clear about the dynamics of return-extrapolative beliefs. First, although beliefs themselves appear to have an autocorrelation coefficient of (1−βS) from Eq. In our model, risk-averse rational traders have forward-looking beliefs about future market returns. However, their beliefs about the future account for the (temporary) impact of extrapolators.
