(20), (21) constitute the state-space system that we choose to estimate in our follow-up analysis.2.3. Model identification and estimationWe impose several parameter constraints to ensure that our model is identified. First, we address the issue that the two loadings Bμ and Bg cannot, in general, be distinguished from the persistence parameters in the model. In this respect, we follow VBK and impose the same parameter constraints in our model. In particular, we set Bg at (1−ρρg)−1. This implies that λg=0 when BS=0 and Bw=0 in the economy studied by VBK, in which gˆt does not affect the expected return. We also set Bμ at (1−ρρμ)−1 so that λμ=(1−ρBSβS)−1. As a result, if extrapolators are absent from the market or are made irrelevant through arbitrage activities (i.e., if BS=0 and Bw=0), λμ collapses to one, and Et[rt+1]=μt. In other words, the model easily accommodates the irrelevance of extrapolators as a special case, and in such a case, the usual result that expected returns equal the discount rate applies. Overall, among the four loadings of our latent processes on the pd ratio, BS and Bw are the only free parameters to be estimated. Both Bμ and Bg are tied to the persistent parameters ρμ and ρg, respectively.Our model is identified after imposing the above parameter constraints. To see this, when BS=0 and Bw=0, extrapolators’ sentiment (i.e., Sˆt and wˆt) disappear from both the pd ratio (i.e., (11)) and market return dynamics (i.e., (12)). Our model collapses to VBK and is thus identified. With BS≠0 or Bw≠0, βS and βw are identified from a projection of the pd ratio on a geometrically weighted average of past returns and a geometrically weighted average of past dividend growth rates, respectively. A simple univariate regression of the pd ratio on lagged returns or lagged dividend growth would not be able to recover extrapolative beliefs correctly, due to an omitted-variable problem. Simply put, such an approach would neglect the co-existence of both return extrapolation and cash-flow extrapolation, leading to biased estimates of either belief formation process. Our structural approach circumvents this issue. At the same time, the intuition for why our structural model is able to simultaneously identify cash-flow extrapolation (henceforth W) and return extrapolation (henceforth S) in the data is simple: while both forms of expectations are weighted sums of past market performance, they are ultimately weighted sums of different quantities. Similarly, the time series of rational expectations of dividend growth can be separated from extrapolative beliefs since it captures investors’ forward-looking expectations of future dividend growth. Finally, the discount rate takes the role of a residual in our decomposition since it is used to match the variation in the price–dividend ratio of the market that cannot be accounted for by either extrapolative beliefs or rational expectations of dividend growth. Since our discount-rate variable effectively is assigned all variation in the price–dividend ratio that cannot be matched by other drivers, it is possible that it captures something other than discount rates. Later, in Section 4, we present an extension of the model that incorporates observable proxies for discount rates in our estimation.To summarize, the parameter vector for our model is Θ=[BS,βS,Bw,βw,ρμ,ρg,σd,σg,σμ]′To maintain model parsimony (since we have only 76 annual observations), in the main model, we do not allow shock correlations.14 All mean parameters are set at their sample means so we can focus on demeaned variables to estimate our model. The state equations are given by (20), and the observation equations are given by (21). We use the standard Kalman-Filtering technique to estimate our model. We detail our estimation procedure in the Appendix, Appendix A.2.4. DataTo perform our analysis, we rely on several sources of data. First and foremost, we use returns on the CRSP value-weighted index from WRDS. These allow us to back out both the pd ratio of the market as well as the monthly dividend payments. We define dividend growth as the year-over-year growth in cumulative 12-month dividends, assuming no dividend reinvestment. Given the seasonality in dividend payments, we follow the present-value literature and focus on market dynamics at the annual frequency. This leads to 76 annual observations in our sample, spanning the 1946 to 2022 period. Our use of 1946 as the starting year is to avoid data errors in the earlier periods, as emphasized by VBK. Given our focus on extrapolation bias, another benefit of using a simple dividend growth process (as opposed to the more complicated market-reinvested dividend growth, for example) is that the dividend process is not confounded with the past return-driven extrapolation process.To validate our novel measure of extrapolative sentiment, we also make use of survey expectations. We focus on surveys that have been used extensively in prior work. These are (i) the American Association of Individual Investors (AAII); (ii) the Gallup Survey of Investor Optimism; (iii) the Investor Intelligence Survey (II); (iv) and surveys of cash-flow extrapolation from Nagel and Xu (2022).15Finally, in studying predictive regressions, we also consider several controls from the return predictability literature that are sourced from Amit Goyal’s website.Given the large number of possible controls and the relatively small number of observations in our sample, we follow the approach in Ludvigson and Ng (2009), who propose the use of principal components in predictive regressions.2.5. Model estimates and implications2.5.1. Model estimatesTable 1 presents the estimation results. Panel A reports results for the model parameters. For parameters common to our paper and VBK, our estimates are close to those reported in VBK. For instance, we estimate ρμ (i.e., persistence in discount rate) to be 0.907, while VBK’s estimate is 0.932. Our estimate of the persistence in the expected dividend growth rate (i.e., ρg) is 0.179, somewhat lower than VBK’s corresponding estimate of 0.354.16 Taking into account the difference in the sample and the modeling framework, we consider our estimates for the baseline parameters close to those obtained in VBK.Next, we turn to parameters that are specific to our model. Our estimate of the parameter βS determining decay in return extrapolative expectations is 0.464, which is highly significant (t-stat =18.56=0.464/0.025). This implies that extrapolators allocate a weight of 0.536 to lagged bias (i.e., Sˆt) and a weight of 0.464 to most recent returns (i.e., rˆt+1), per (6). The coefficient estimate suggests that recent returns matter considerably for return-extrapolative beliefs and that lagged yearly returns matter about half as much as the most recent ones. Thus, this result is qualitatively consistent with Greenwood and Shleifer (2014) evidence of return extrapolation from surveys of returns. The key parameter that captures the loading of the pd ratio on the extrapolators’ sentiment, BS, is estimated to be 0.100 and is highly statistically significant (t-stat =4.20=0.1/0.0238). A positive BS implies that positive sentiment is associated with an inflated pd ratio, consistent with the role of return extrapolation in theory work by Barberis et al. (2015).Turning to cash-flow extrapolation, our estimate of βw, the parameter determining decay in cash-flow extrapolative expectations, is 0.0004, which is small and not statistically different from zero. This implies that cash-flow extrapolators’ beliefs have a much stronger persistence than the beliefs of return extrapolators and that the relevant history of past cash flows that cash-flow extrapolators rely on is much longer than the history of past returns that enters return-extrapolative beliefs. This result resonates with the evidence of Nagel and Xu (2022), who point out the much longer memory that is embedded in cash-flow extrapolative beliefs vis-a-vis return-extrapolation. As far as the loading of cash-flow extrapolation on the price–dividend ratio Bw, it is large and highly statistically significant (t-stat 5.70 = 232.70/40.80), underscoring the importance of cash-flow extrapolation in rationalizing variation in the price–dividend ratio.The other estimated parameters reported in Table 1 are consistent with the intuition of our model. For example, both Bμ and Bg are estimated to be positive. This result implies a negative (positive) response of the pd ratio to discount rate (dividend-growth) news, respectively. As another example, as in (13), the expected one-year market return loads positively on the discount rate (through λμ), while it loads negatively on return extrapolators’ sentiment (through a negative value of λS) and on cash-flow extrapolators’ sentiment (through a negative value of λw), and positively on dividend news. Note that both λS and λw are statistically significant, hinting at a role of extrapolation in predictive regressions. We examine such a role in detail later in the paper.In Panel C of Table 1, we investigate how important the different sources of variation in the pd ratio are. When we ask what is the contribution of, say, cash-flow extrapolators, to the variance of aggregate valuations, we are inherently asking by how much the variance of the price–dividend ratio would change relative to the one observed in the data in the counterfactual scenario where there are no cash-flow extrapolators in the market. To answer this question, we consider, as it is standard in other applications of structural estimation in finance, a counterfactual scenario in which cash-flow extrapolators have left the market. This scenario corresponds to measuring the variance of the pd ratio that is implied by our model when Bw=0. In such a counterfactual scenario, we find that the variance of the pd ratio would drop by close to 20%. This 20% contribution of cash-flow extrapolation to the variance of the pd ratio is remarkably close to the simulation results in Nagel and Xu (2022), Table 5.17When applying the same approach to return extrapolation, our results are also broadly in line with Nagel and Xu (2022). They argue that return extrapolation is unlikely to account for much of the variation in the log price–dividend ratio, owing to the transitory nature of the former, which makes it not a good candidate to explain the latter. Consistent with them, we find that a counterfactual scenario in which return extrapolators have left the market (BS=0) implies a very small decline in the variance of the price–dividend ratio. Of course, this need not mean that return extrapolators do not matter. In fact, it is possible that there are periods of intensified extrapolation in which, for instance, the degree of extrapolation is high and the dynamics of the dividend–price ratio change considerably as a result of return-extrapolative beliefs (e.g., Cassella and Gulen 2018). These are, however, conditional effects that our approach, estimating unconditional model parameters and full-sample relationships, would not be able to account for. Finally, when considering discount rates and rational expectations of cash-flow growth, our approach suggests that discount rates are important, in that the variance of the price–dividend ratio would decline by more than 90% with constant discount rates, while the importance of rational cash-flow expectations is much more modest. These results accord well with past work (e.g., Van Binsbergen and Koijen 2010).2.5.2. Impulse responseWe also examine our model estimates through the lens of impulse responses. This part of the analysis allows us to compare the insights of our econometric models with the predictions of theories of extrapolation in the market.Fig. 1 plots the impulse responses for both the state variables (Sˆt, wˆt μˆt, and gˆt) and the model-implied quantities (pd̂t, Et(rˆt+1), and rˆt) between one and five years after a one-standard-deviation positive shock in one of the state variables at time 0. Panels A and B report dynamics following shocks to return extrapolation. Panels C and D report dynamics following shocks to cash flow extrapolation. Panels E and F report dynamics following shocks to the discount rate, and Panels G and H present dynamics following shocks to rational expectations of dividend growth. The response variables are also standardized with respect to their respective standard deviations.For brevity, we concentrate on shocks to extrapolation. As Fig. 1 shows, a positive shock in return extrapolation at time 0 implies an increase in the price–dividend ratio. Such an increase is rather modest, and it reverts to the mean quickly due to the relatively high value of the structural parameter βS governing return extrapolation. Moreover, an increase in return extrapolation leads to lower expected and realized returns in the future. Finally, a movement in return extrapolation does not per se generate changes to the other state variables. When comparing the implications of return-extrapolation shocks and cash-flow extrapolation shocks for returns and the pd ratio, there are several similarities, but there are also important differences. First, an increase in extrapolative beliefs about cash flows has a spillover effect on return extrapolation, in that an increase in cash-flow extrapolation raises prices today. Such an increase boosts return extrapolation today, but lowers extrapolation in the future due to the negative relation between cash-flow extrapolation and future returns.18 Second, since cash-flow extrapolation is a rather persistent process, a shock today leads cash flow extrapolation to remain high for a long time in the future. This high persistence in extrapolative beliefs about cash flows generates persistence in the behavior of the log price–dividend ratio, which grows following a rise in cash flow extrapolation and remains high for many years afterward.Shocks to discount rates cause a decline in prices and returns today, leading to lower return extrapolation today and a slow increase in the future, as higher discount rates turn into higher future returns on average. On the other hand, discount rates have no impact on cash flow extrapolation. Finally, in our model, shocks to expectations of dividend growth lead to an increase in prices today, as in the traditional present-value relation. However, due to the low persistence in g, the impact on the price–dividend ratio is rather mild, and so is the impact on asset returns, leading to only a small response by return extrapolation. The main takeaway of this part of the analysis is that shocks to different state variables will have different implications for the behavior of asset valuation, returns, and expected returns.2.6. Model extensions2.6.1. Incorporating observable proxies for discount ratesOne limitation of our approach concerns the modeling of the discount rate. In our model, the discount rate process is identified from the variation in the price–dividend ratio of the market that cannot be accounted for with the other three latent processes that we consider. As such, what we refer to as a discount rate can capture drivers of asset valuations that are rather distinct from risk preferences. One solution to this problem is to extend our model to explicitly link variation in the discount-rate process to some observable proxy for risk preferences. We pursue this solution and present, in the Internet Appendix, Section IA1, an extended model that incorporates observable metrics for discount rates in our analysis. We discuss the implications on predictive regressions of this extended model in Section 4.2.6.2. Correlated innovationsOne caveat in interpreting our main results is that our model shuts down potential correlations in innovations to maintain model parsimony. While this approach eases estimation, it may lead to oversimplified dynamics. In our Internet Appendix Section IA1 we present an extended version of our model that allows shock correlations in the state variables. There, we also report our model estimates. These estimates do not differ much from the estimates in our main analysis. We discuss the implications on predictive regressions of this extended model in Section 4.
article.3. Validating the model: The purpose of this section is to investigate the properties of our newly estimated time series of extrapolative expectations, S and W. Since our approach to obtaining extrapolative beliefs is based on prices rather than surveys, we are particularly interested in how well our model can recover underlying unobservable extrapolative beliefs. To this end, we perform three pieces of analysis. In Section 3.1, we present the results of simulations aimed at assessing how well our model is able to jointly recover extrapolative beliefs alongside discount rates and rational expectations of dividend growth for a broad set of possible model parameters. In Section 3.2 we use another form of validation of our approach to measuring extrapolative beliefs through prices. In particular, having estimated our model based on the US stock market, we can rely on several surveys collected in the US and ask how well our structural measures of extrapolation track survey-based extrapolative metrics. Finally, Section 3.3 investigates the predictive properties of our price-based gauges of extrapolative beliefs for future returns. Like in the previous section, the focus is on comparing the ability to predict future returns using survey-based measures with the ability to do so using our price-based metrics for extrapolation.3.1. Simulation analysis3.1.1. SetupTo study the performance of the Kalman-Filtering-based estimation in the context of our model of Section 2.2, we conduct an extensive simulation analysis. We first set the true population parameters at Θ̂—the parameter estimates given in Table 1, Panel A. For each simulation run (indexed by d, d=1,2,…,D=500), we start from the steady state; that is, all state variables are set at their unconditional means (or, equivalently, all demeaned state variables are set at zero). We simulate 76 years of data by randomly generating normally and independently (both cross-sectionally and across time) distributed shocks for the three innovations in our model (i.e., ɛμ,t+1, ɛg,t+1, and ɛd,t+1). Ultimately, in each simulation, we create an artificial time series of the pd ratio based on Eq. (11). We then use the same Kalman-filtering techniques used in the analysis of the actual data in the paper to extract the latent processes in our model. We repeat the simulation 500 times so as to obtain a mean and median pairwise correlation between the latent variables created as part of the simulations and the latent time series filtered from the data.We base our main simulations on the model parameters estimated from the data in Table 1. Of course, these parameters are only estimates, and we are interested in how robust our methodology is to different values of model parameters. Therefore, we probe our results for robustness to alternative parameterizations of the data. Since there are many model parameters, it is difficult to experiment with all possible combinations of such parameters. To offer a parsimonious description of the simulation results, we focus on varying only a subset of model parameters. We argue that, for some parameters, such as the persistence of the discount-rate process or the persistence of the rational dividend-growth expectations, our estimates align fairly well with estimates from other studies. Thus, there is less of a need to perform simulations with alternative values of these parameters. Instead, given that the novelty of our approach lies in incorporating extrapolative beliefs in the present-value relation, we perform simulations for different combinations of the extrapolators’ decay parameters βS and βw. In some of these simulations, we make the two parameters closer to each other than in the data. Effectively, this amounts to changing the persistence of these two series so as to ascertain how much the success of our model depends on differences in the time-series properties of these two distinct latent processes. We are also interested in how robust the identification of different latent processes is to the nature of the shocks in our model, particularly with respect to the heterogeneity in the variance of such shocks. To this end, unlike in the data, in all our additional simulations, we assume the three shocks in our system to have an equal standard deviation of 1%.3.1.2. Simulation resultsTable 3 shows the results of the simulations. In the Table, the column labeled “Data” is meant to represent the results of simulations involving the exact values of model parameters that are estimated from the data in Table 1. All other statistics refer to additional simulations where σμ=σd=σg=1%, ρμ=0.9, ρg=0.2, BS=0.1, and Bw=200, and where extrapolation weight parameters are varied as indicated on the top of the Table.The table shows that all latent time series can be jointly recovered by our estimation procedure quite accurately. Across different parameterizations of the model, the mean (median) correlation between the actual discount rate and the recovered one is around 81%. Similarly, the mean (median) correlation between actual and estimated return-extrapolation sentiment is around 83%, while it is 90% for cash-flow extrapolation. Results are very similar when focusing only on the data-based parameterizations of the model. Overall, the results of the simulation showcase the ability of our model to jointly recover the latent variables in our augmented present-value identity.3.2. Relation between survey-based and structurally estimated extrapolation metricsAside from using simulation analysis to validate our estimation, we also ask how well our price-based measures of extrapolation match the survey data. Table 2 reports pairwise correlations between our price-based return extrapolation metric S and three widely used surveys of return expectations, the American Association of Individual Investors Survey (AAII), the Gallup Survey of Investor Optimism (GALLUP), and the Investor Intelligence Surveys (II). We also construct a combination of these three time-series, which we refer to as SURVEYSRET.19Across surveys, and as the left picture of Fig. 2, Panel A confirms, the correlation between S and survey-based expectations is generally positive. While the correlation between S and raw survey data on return expectations is positive, it is also the case that not all variation in the time series of survey expectations comes from return extrapolation. For instance, Greenwood and Shleifer (2014), Table 4, estimates a regression of survey expectations on a weighted sum of past stock returns. Across surveys, the average R2 of this model is only 37.5%, indicating that there is a large fraction of the variation in survey beliefs that is unrelated to extrapolation. Thus, we argue that a more informative comparison is between S and the extrapolative component of surveys of return expectations,20 To that end, we also construct a survey-based extrapolation time series that is the more relevant survey-based counterpart to our structurally estimated return-extrapolation measure. To this end, we follow Greenwood and Shleifer (2014), who gauge the extrapolative nature of survey expectations by means of a regression of survey expectations on past stock market returns R(t−j): (22)SURVEYSRETt=a+b∑j=160wjR(t−j+1)+ϵt,wj=(λ)(j−1)∑k=1N(λ)(k−1). The left-hand side is represented by the survey expectations principal component SURVEYSRET, which is available between 1996 and 2011 at the monthly frequency. On the right-hand side, the weight assigned to j-quarter lagged returns, wj, accommodates the possibility that recent returns have a larger impact on expectations. Like prior literature, we set N=60, i.e., we use 15 years of past return history. Then we use the in-sample parameter estimates {aˆ,bˆ,λˆ} jointly with past stock market returns to construct a long time series of extrapolative beliefs that span the sample period 1946–2022. We refer to the resulting time-series as SURVEYSRETX. When we estimate the correlation between S and SURVEYSRETX in Table 2, we find it is almost 70%, underscoring the high degree to which our price-based extrapolation measure resembles the survey-based direct measure of extrapolation. This high correlation is evident in the left graph Fig. 2, Panel B.We perform a similar comparison concerning cash flow extrapolation. We consider two relevant comparisons in this case. The first comparison is between our structural measure of cash flow extrapolation W and raw survey data on cash flow expectations from Nagel and Xu (2022). We refer to this time series as SURVEYSCF. The second comparison concerns the extrapolative component of cash-flow expectations, which we label SURVEYSCFX, and which is obtained based on the model of asset pricing with fading memory of Nagel and Xu (2022).21 We find our price-based extrapolation metric to be highly correlated with both the cash flow expectations survey (59%) and its extrapolation component (46%). The two right figures in Panel A and Panel B of Fig. 2 speak to the high degree of comovement between our price-based cash flow extrapolation metric and its survey-based counterpart.To complement the analysis above, Fig. 2 Panel C overlays the price–dividend ratio of the market with our price-based extrapolation time series. The figure shows the high degree of correlation between cash-flow extrapolation and the pd ratio (79%), while the correlation of the pd ratio with return extrapolation is rather small (below 1%), resonating with prior evidence (e.g., Nagel and Xu 2022). In sum, the correlation analysis indicates that S and w are consistent with the evidence of extrapolative expectations from surveys.3.3. Return predictability testsOne key tenet of theories of extrapolation in financial markets is that extrapolative beliefs lead to mispricing and thus hold a negative relation with future market returns. This is also an outcome in our model, as shown earlier in (13). So far, the evidence of predictability by extrapolative beliefs in the literature is rather mixed.22 The evidence is also fragmented in that some studies analyze the predictive properties of return expectations, while others examine the role of cash-flow expectations for returns. This fragmentation can, in part, be due to the fact that data on return expectations and cash flow expectations is often collected using heterogeneous means and survey populations or over heterogeneous sample periods, making it difficult to conduct a unified assessment of the predictive properties of extrapolative beliefs for future returns. The aim of this section is to use our estimated extrapolation time series to test for the role of extrapolation in return predictability. Our primary goal is to compare the performance of our price-based extrapolation measures with the survey-based measures. Our second goal is to offer a comparison of the predictive ability of return extrapolation and cash flow extrapolation in a setting where the measurement of both processes happens jointly.We begin by performing, in Table 4, in-sample predictability tests of future aggregate stock returns for horizons of 3 months (Panel A), 12 months (Panel B), and 36 months (Panel C). In the table, we regress future annualized log cumulative market returns on our four time series: S (return extrapolation), μ (rational expectations), g (expected dividend growth), and w (cashflow extrapolation). In all tests, we standardize the regressors to a mean of zero and a standard deviation of one. We use μ and g as estimated by our model, while S and w are either sourced from surveys (SURVEYSRETX and SURVEYCFX, respectively) or from our own structural estimation as indicated in the heading of each panel. Since regressions are run using yearly data, some of our results are based on the use of overlapping observations. Therefore, we use a block-bootstrapping approach for inference. In addition to the OLS coefficient estimates, we also report bootstrapped 95% confidence intervals and bootstrapped p-values. For our main variables of interest, S and w, we are interested in testing the null of no predictability against the one-sided behavioral hypothesis that over-reaction in extrapolative beliefs is followed by correction and lower returns. To this end, we also report one-sided left-tail bootstrapped p-values. The table measures each model’s goodness-of-fit by means of the adjusted R2 statistic, which allows us to compare the performance of different models in the presence of a different number of regressors across models. Moreover, in each of the regressions where we include extrapolative beliefs on the right-hand side, we test the hypothesis that extrapolative beliefs have no predictive power for future returns by means of an F-test of the joint hypothesis that the predictability coefficients of S and w are jointly equal to zero.Column (1) in each panel of Table 4 provides estimation results for a baseline specification that only contains μ and g. In Columns (2) and (5), we predict returns in a regression that only includes extrapolative beliefs. In Columns (3) and (6), we estimate a predictive model featuring the four latent processes estimated as part of our model. Finally, Columns (4) and (7) augment the full specification with a control variable that, in the spirit of Ludvigson and Ng (2009), is the first principal component of a large set of time-series predictors of future stock returns.23We start by describing the results of quarter-ahead predictability regressions. In Column (1), neither μ nor g predict future stock returns at the standard level of statistical significance. Moreover, the adjusted R2 is small. In Columns (2) and (5), we find evidence of short-term predictability by cash flow extrapolation. Return extrapolation loads negatively on future returns, but the predictive coefficient is not statistically significant at conventional levels. The predictive properties of survey-based and price-based extrapolation metrics are rather similar, although the model using price-based metrics is better able at matching variation of future returns, with an R2 that is almost three times as large as the one obtained when using survey-based extrapolation metrics. When estimating the full model in Columns (3) and (6), we notice a decline in the adjusted R2, and the loadings on μ and g remain not statistically significant. Moreover, whereas when using survey data, there is no statistical evidence of a relationship between extrapolation and future returns, our price-based return extrapolation proxy is a significant negative predictor of future returns with a one-sided p-value of 0.08, and cash flow extrapolation is significantly negatively linked to future returns with a one-sided p-value of 0.01. In addition, when using our price-based extrapolation proxies in Column (5), the null of no role of extrapolation for future returns is rejected at the 5% level of significance. The predictability by S and w becomes weaker when introducing controls, although cash flow extrapolation appears to still predict returns with a one-sided p-value of 0.04 when we measure extrapolation using our approach.Overall, the quarter-ahead results underscore the similar predictive properties of survey-based metrics and price-based proxies for extrapolation, and they provide suggestive evidence that the latter offer stronger predictive power for future returns than the former. This evidence is reinforced in Panel B of Table 4, in which we perform the predictability analysis at the yearly forecasting horizon. These tests show that μ has predictive power for future returns. μ retains its predictive power when we introduce survey-based extrapolators metrics in the regression, but it becomes insignificant when we control for our price-based extrapolation measures. Moreover, while both survey-based and proxy-based return-extrapolation measures are significant predictors of future returns, cash-flow extrapolation has predictive power for future returns only when it is measured via our price-based proxy. The joint significance of price-based cashflow- and return-extrapolation measures in predicting returns is underscored by the F-test of joint significance, which, across specifications, rejects the null of no predictability by extrapolation with an average p-value of 0.04. Finally, the overall goodness of fit of the model is higher when using price-based proxies. For instance, when comparing Columns (2) and (5), where we predict returns only using extrapolative beliefs, the adjusted R2 is 4% if we use survey-based extrapolation measures, but it rises to 11% with our filtered time series of extrapolative beliefs.In summary, the results of the predictive tests at the yearly horizon strengthen the view that survey-based and price-based measures of extrapolation have similar predictive properties and that price-based measures can be somewhat more successful at predicting returns at the intermediate prediction horizon of a year. The last set of results concerns the long-term predictability of 3-year ahead returns. In this last set of tests, the predictive power of the discount rate strengthens considerably across specifications. Moreover, we note that there is stronger evidence of return predictability by survey-based return extrapolation as opposed to our price-based return extrapolation, while the evidence in favor of the predictive power of cash flow extrapolation is comparable across metrics. Overall, across horizons, our results indicate that survey-based and price-based extrapolation proxies share similar predictive properties. The results also indicate that there may be benefits in integrating these alternative metrics into a unified measure. This is an interesting topic that we leave to future research.Lastly, as far as the relative importance of return extrapolation and cash-flow extrapolation for the predictability of returns is concerned, our results indicate that both are important. The size of the predictability, represented by the magnitude of the predictability coefficients, is quite comparable, and there are several specifications in which both cash flow extrapolation and return extrapolation exhibit predictive power for future returns. There is also some evidence that predictability by return extrapolation may be stronger at short and intermediate horizons and be weak or absent at longer horizons. In contrast, cash flow extrapolation appears to predict returns at all prediction horizons.3.4. Out-of-sample predictabilityWe complement the in-sample analysis of the previous section with out-of-sample (OOS) predictability statistics. These are Campbell and Thompson (2008) and Goyal and Welch (2008) out-of-sample R2, and Clark and West (2007) statistic for the comparison of the out-of-sample performance of nested models. When comparing the out-of-sample fit of two predictive models, Mj and Mk, over a sample of T observations, the OOS R2 is obtained as (23)ROOS(j,k)2=1−∑t=0T−1(rt+1−rkt̂)2∑t=0T−1(rt+1−rjt̂)2,where rt+1 is the next-period realized return, rkt̂ is the prediction of rt+1 that model Mk casts at time t, and rjt̂ is the prediction of rt+1 made by model Mj. A positive value of ROOS(j,k)2 indicates that the out-of-sample mean-squared forecasting error obtained under model Mk is smaller than the mean-squared forecasting error of model Mj, and, hence, Mk has a better out-of-sample performance than Mj. Conversely, a negative value of ROOS(j,k)2 suggests better out-of-sample predictability is obtained using model Mj.Having outlined in (13) the Equation for expected returns in our model, we consider three models of returns that are relevant in our setting: M0:rt+1=a+ϵ0(t+1)M1:rt+1=a+b(λμμˆt+λggˆt)+ϵ1(t+1)M2:rt+1=a+b(λμμˆt+λggˆt+λSSˆt+λwwˆt)+ϵ2(t+1)Model M0 is the naive forecast, i.e., the prediction that the future stock market return is equal to the historical average. Model M1 is a model of future returns that only relies on discount rates (μt) and expected dividend growth (gt) to predict future returns but neglects extrapolative expectations. In other words, M1 is a model where we restrict λS and λw in (13) to be equal to zero. M2 is the full model that augments M1 with St and wt, as per (13).When considering model M2, we follow the same approach adopted in the in-sample tests of Table 4, in that we present results both when S and w are structurally estimated and when they are based on survey data.Each model’s time-t forecast of rt+1 is based on the in-sample parameter estimates obtained with the data available between the sample start date (i.e., 1946) and time t−1. These estimates are then used jointly with the values of the model regressors a and b at time t to cast an out-of-sample one-period ahead prediction.24 Once all forecast errors are available, they are used in the calculation of the out-of-sample R2 as in (23). VBK set the minimum in-sample size to be equal to half the sample. On the other hand, since the out-of-sample R2 is sensitive to the choice of the out-of-sample period, we report results for two alternative OOS start dates, 1973 (i.e., first out-of-sample estimation based on 25 years of in-sample data) and 1998 (i.e., first out-of-sample estimation based on 50 years of in-sample data).The results of this part of the analysis are in Panel A of Table 5. Like VBK, we concentrate on out-of-sample predictability of year-ahead cumulative log market returns. In the table, the column labeled M1vsM0 reports the out-of-sample R2 of the bivariate model vis-a-vis the naive forecasting. Similarly, M2vsM0 refers to a comparison of the full model with the naive forecast. Finally, to gauge the benefit of modeling an extrapolative expectations component in addition to discount rates and expected dividend growth, we also report results for the comparison M2vsM1.We consider two types of out-of-sample statistics. The first, labeled as “FS”, is based on filtering over the full sample (1946 to 2022). The second, labeled “OOS”, takes a more conservative approach, as it is based on the econometrician’s use of the recursively estimated model parameters to back out the time-t values of the filtered time-series based on information available up to time t only.25We start by describing the comparison M2vsM1, which is the most relevant case in light of the in-sample results reported earlier. We first focus on the results of the analysis based on our structural estimates of extrapolative beliefs. Across different out-of-sample starting dates and different measurements of the filtered states, the out-of-sample R2 is consistently positive and large, ranging from 0.76% to about 2.42%. This resonates with our in-sample finding on the usefulness of incorporating extrapolative expectations into a model of expected returns. These results are qualitatively similar to the ones that we obtain when using survey-based extrapolation metrics as opposed to our structurally estimated measures. This result further reinforces the view that our approach is able to render an accurate representation of extrapolative beliefs in the market.The second metric we consider is Clark and West (2007) statistic. The statistic is used to compare the out-of-sample forecasting accuracy of a linear predictive model (Mk) with the accuracy of a more parsimonious model Mj where some of the predictability coefficients are restricted to be equal to 0. Since this statistic is meant to be used for nested models, we can use it to compare M0 with M1 and with M2, but we cannot use it to compare M1 with M2, since neither model nests the other one.The statistic is formally defined as CWOOS(j,k)=21T∑t=0T−1FEjt[FEjt−FEkt],where FEjt and FEkt are the forecast errors of model Mj and model Mk, respectively. Under the null, the parsimonious model generates the data, and hence model Mk will generate, on average larger mean-squared out-of-sample forecast errors (FE) than model Mj, i.e., CWOOS is negative. Under the alternative hypothesis, the out-of-sample forecast errors from the augmented model Mk are smaller than the forecast errors from model Mj, i.e., CWOOS is positive. Moreover, Clark and West (2007) show that testing the alternative hypothesis of a higher forecasting accuracy by model Mk can be accomplished by regressing the time-series of FEjt[FEjt−FEkt] on a constant and comparing the obtained t-statistic with standard one-sided critical values of 1.28 (10% rejection rate), 1.64 (5% rejection rate), or 1.96 (1% rejection rate).Panel B of Table 5 reports the results for this part of the analysis. We focus on M2vsM0, that is, the comparison between the bivariate model that neglects extrapolative beliefs, and the full model that accounts for such beliefs. Across the eight different versions of the test, we find that the Clark and West (2007) statistic is always positive. This indicates that incorporating extrapolative expectations into a model of short-term market returns is useful. Moreover, echoing the results involving the OOS R2, the performance of the models is rather comparable when using our structural approach to recovering beliefs and when using survey-based measures of beliefs. Overall, these results give us additional confidence about the ability of our model to recover beliefs from market valuations.
article.4. Model extensions and discussion: The most straightforward extension of our baseline model is to allow the innovations in Eq. (15) to be cross-correlated. We present this extended model in the Internet Appendix, Section IA1, where we refer to this model as Mcce. The second extension concerns modeling discount rates by incorporating some observable proxy for risk preferences in the model. This second model, which we refer to as MZ, is also presented in detail in Section IA1 of the Internet Appendix. Table A3.1 in the Appendix shows pairwise correlations between the estimates of return extrapolation obtained using our main model of Section 2, as well as Mcce and MZ. We find the time series of extrapolative beliefs extracted from the main model of Section 2.2 and the time series of extrapolative beliefs obtained from the extended models to be more than 99% correlated. This result underscores the robustness of our results. Table IA.2 in the Internet Appendix also repeats the return-predictability tests using the alternative measures of extrapolative beliefs from Mcce and MZ. The results are similar to the one in our main Table 4.4.1. DiscussionOur approach to measuring extrapolative beliefs from market prices has three main benefits. First and foremost, our methodology does not rely on survey data, whose coverage is often limited to only a small set of investors, some sample periods, and some markets and countries. In contrast, since our estimation only relies on observable market quantities (i.e., pd ratios and market returns), the approach proposed in this paper is portable and can be applied to multiple settings where surveys are lacking. Second and related, several researchers have expressed concerns in the past over the use of surveys in asset pricing tests due to the potential lack of quality of survey data and a limited time-series sample. Our market-based approach circumvents these concerns because we extract investors’ beliefs from prices. Third, surveys do not only measure extrapolative beliefs but also other biases. By comparison, our structural approach imposes economically motivated functional forms for extrapolative beliefs, thus allowing us to recover a component of beliefs that is only due to extrapolation as opposed to other sources of deviations from rationality. Of course, this strength of our approach also represents a limitation: if other drivers of beliefs exist that lead to deviations from rationality and mispricing, then our model is going to miss these drivers, and the relevance of extrapolation for aggregate valuation may not be fully representative of how much, as a whole, biases in beliefs matter in the market. There is recent evidence that biases in beliefs other than extrapolation can affect asset prices in the aggregate (e.g., Bordalo et al. 2024). For this reason, we caution the reader that the survey-based approach does not only entail difficulties but can rather offer advantages that the narrower scope of our approach cannot easily match.4.2. Preferences or beliefsSetting aside the benefits of our approach, we acknowledge there are also some reasons why, if survey data was available in real time for any given market of interest, one could prefer to test behavioral theories using surveys. The main reason why surveys could be preferred over a market-based measure of beliefs is that there is a widely held view in financial economics that, given market prices alone, we cannot separate preferences and beliefs. Thus, when recovering beliefs from prices, one must be concerned that some of the variation in extrapolative beliefs that we measure is not, in fact, due to incorrect beliefs but may instead stem from some preferences. With respect to this concern, we notice that structurally estimating a very specific functional form for extrapolative beliefs in the way we do may, to some extent, help alleviate this concern. Moreover, our structurally estimated extrapolation time-series accord well with survey-based data on investor beliefs about future fundamentals and future returns. That said, we acknowledge the importance of surveys as a direct gauge of survey beliefs, and we leave the task of integrating survey data with our structural estimates of extrapolative beliefs to future research.
article.5. Conclusion: The question of whether behavioral biases matter in financial markets is one of the most debated issues in academic finance. On the one hand, a rational expectations view of the market dismisses biases as irrelevant. This is either because biases are idiosyncratic and hence disappear in the aggregate or because arbitrage forces quickly correct any mispricing induced by behavioral agents, thus marginalizing the role of biases for price formation. The alternative view is that arbitrage faces severe constraints and that biases are not idiosyncratic. Rather, biases are driven by common heuristic rules of belief formation, which systematically lead investors to deviate from rational expectations and lead prices to deviate from fundamentals.This paper provides a key contribution to this extant debate as we set out to assess the role of a common source of biased beliefs in the equity market, namely, extrapolation for stock prices. Existing work that attempts to shed light on the role of extrapolation in the market is fraught with empirical limitations. The paucity of long-running surveys on expectations, as well as the lack of a direct connection between survey data on beliefs, investor behavior, and asset prices, hinders researchers’ ability to test behavioral theories. Our novel approach addresses these limitations head-on as we aim to extract the time series of extrapolative beliefs in the marketplace directly from asset prices. Our methodology inherently nests the traditional finance view as a special case and identifies biases only to the extent that these biases matter.Our results provide evidence consistent with recent behavioral theories of asset prices. In these theories, some investors’ extrapolative beliefs generate temporary deviations of prices from the level warranted by fundamentals, and the predictability of asset returns arises as a result. Aside from its direct contribution to the extrapolation literature, we take our methodological and empirical contribution as the first step toward a new stream of literature that tests behavioral theories of asset prices without relying solely on survey data or data on investor behavior. We demonstrate that by imposing a theory-based structure on the belief formation process of interest, one can recover evidence and variation in biases purely from prices. We believe our proposed methodological shift unlocks many promising avenues for future research in behavioral finance.
